content.problemStatement: Given a list of strings **S**[ 1..**N** ], you need to count how many ordered pairs of strings are awesome. Given two integers i and j, such that 1 ≤ i, j ≤ n and i ≠ j, an ordered pair ( **S**[ **i** ], **S**[ **j** ] ) is called awesome if and only if concatenating **S**[ **i** ] and **S**[ **j** ] gives a palindrome.
constraints: Sum of lengths of all strings in a test case ≤10^6
Strings contain only lowercase English letters; the empty string is allowed.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of strings. Then N lines follow, each containing a string of lowercase English letters; a line may be empty representing the empty string.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of ordered pairs (i, j) with i≠j such that the concatenation S[i]+S[j] is a palindrome.

solution.approach: Store the frequency of every distinct string in an unordered_map<string,long long>. For each distinct string s we pre‑compute two boolean arrays: prefPal[i] is true if the prefix s[0..i] is a palindrome, and suffPal[i] is true if the suffix s[i..len-1] is a palindrome. For every split position pos (0…len) we have prefix = s[0..pos-1] and suffix = s[pos..len-1]. If prefPal[pos-1] is true (or pos==0) then any string t whose reverse equals suffix will form a palindrome when placed before s, because reverse(suffix)+s = reverse(suffix)+prefix+suffix is a palindrome. Similarly, if suffPal[pos] is true (or pos==len) then any string t whose reverse equals prefix will form a palindrome when placed after s. Using the map we obtain the count of such t in O(1). While adding contributions we subtract the case where t is the same occurrence of s (i=j). Summing over all distinct strings and multiplying by their frequencies yields the total number of ordered pairs. The algorithm processes each string in O(L) time where L is its length, so the total time is O(total length + U·M) ≤ O(10^6·100) which fits the limits. Memory usage is O(U·average length) for storing the strings and O(U) for the map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(totalLength + U·M) ≤ O(10^8) per test case, where totalLength is the sum of lengths of all strings, U is the number of distinct strings and M≤100 is the maximum string length.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(totalLength) for storing the input strings plus O(U) for the hash map of frequencies.
